rulings.Case Title: CITY OF SAN JOSE VS. STATE HISTORICAL RESOURCES COMMISSION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Real Party In Interest Friends Of The Willow Glen Trestle Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees (Part 2 Of 3) (Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 1, Real Party In Interest Friends Of The Willow Glen Trestle Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees (Part 2 Of 3) (for tentative Ruling Only) Here, Real Party in Interest did not succeed on any significant issue in this litigation. (Cf. Save Our Heritage Organisation, 11 Cal.App.5th at 157 [project proponent successfully appealed judgment granting petition for writ of mandate which challenged city's approval of site development permit for park revitalization project]; Committee to Defend Reproductive Rights v. A Free Pregnancy Center (1991) 229 Cal.App.3d 633, 635-637 [appellants filed initial complaint in San Francisco Superior Court, later consolidated with similar action brought by district attorney, resulting in judgment against respondents].) Rather, it was unsuccessful in opposing the City's petition for writ of mandate, which sought to set aside the Commission's listing decision. Over the Commission's and Real Party in Interest's opposition, the Court agreed in part with the City's position, finding that the Commission had failed to make required statutory findings, and remanded the matter to the Commission to make revised findings; after it had done so, the Court denied the City's petition. To be sure, the net effect of the revised findings was to reaffirm the Commission's prior listing decision, which Real Party supports. However, Real Party does not seek attorney's fees for its role in nominating the Trestle for listing or for participating in the proceedings before the Commission, but rather for its minor role in the largely inconclusive litigation before this Court, which effectively left unchanged the Commission's original decision. Likewise, the pendency of the separate CEQA litigation in Santa Clara County and the Sixth District Court of Appeal concerning the City's proposal to demolish and replace the Trestle is irrelevant to the current motion. Because Real Party did not prevail on any significant issue in this litigation, and its limited participation did not result in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest, its motion must be denied. (See, e.g., Morgan v. Imperial Irrigation Dist. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 892, 929 ["the court does not have the discretion to award [section 1021.5] fees unless the statutory criteria have been met as a matter of law"].) Finally, Real Party does not show that private enforcement was necessary, as the Commission was already fully and competently represented by the Attorney General. The "necessity of private enforcement" prong of section 1021.5 "'looks to the adequacy of public enforcement and seeks economic equalization of representation in cases where private enforcement is necessary.'" (Conservatorship of Wiley (2010) 50 Cal.4th 1206, 1215.) Thus, an award of attorney's fees under section 1021.5 is "not appropriate where the public rights in question were adequately vindicated by governmental action." (Id.; see also Bui v. Nguyen (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 1357, 1373 ["When a court 'looks to the adequacy of public enforcement' [citation omitted], it must consider whether the evidence shows that public enforcement is inadequate"]; City of Sacramento v. Drew (1989) 207 Cal.App.3d 1287, 1299 ["Thus, if there is a public attorney general available to enforce the important right at issue there is no utility in inducing a private attorney general to duplicate the function."]; County of Humboldt v. Swoap (1975) 51 Cal.App.3d 442, 445 ["the courts should insure that a recipient's legal representative is not compensated for making a merely nominal appearance or by duplicating the efforts of the Attorney General in representing the director."].) (end of part 2 of 3) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CITY OF SAN JOSE VS. STATE HISTORICAL RESOURCES COMMISSION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 1, REAL PARTY IN INTEREST FRIENDS OF THE WILLOW GLEN TRESTLE's Motion For Attorneys Fees. (part 1 of 3) Real Party in Interest Friends of the Willow Glen Trestle's motion for an award of attorney's fees under Code of Civil Procedure section 1021.5 is denied. Petitioner City of San José brought this action seeking a petition for writ of mandate directing Respondent State Historical Resources Commission to set aside its May 10, 2017 decision, which it declared to be final on October 27, 2017, to list the Willow Glen Trestle on the California Register of Historical Resources. Movant Friends of the Willow Glen Trestle, which had nominated the trestle for listing, was named in the petition as the real party in interest. The Commission, represented by the Attorney General, opposed the petition. Movant joined in that opposition, as well as filing its own short opposition, arguing that the petition was "wholly without merit and should be denied." (2/15/19 Opp. at 5.) After a hearing held on March 7, 2019, the Court issued its tentative ruling granting the City's petition for writ of mandate. On April 2, 2019, the Court issued an order for interlocutory remand, finding that the Commission had abused its discretion by failing to proceed in accordance with the requirement in Public Resources Code § 5024.1(f)(3) and 14 Code Cal. Regs. § 4855(b)(2) that it make findings that "shall identify . . . the overriding significance of the resource that has resulted in the resource being listed in the California Register over the objections of the local government." The Court remanded the matter to the Commission to clarify or revise its findings, and retained jurisdiction over the City's petition pending such further findings. In its order, the Court expressly noted that the Commission's finding that the Trestle meets the criteria for listing in the California Register was supported by substantial evidence and was not challenged by the City. On May 21, 2019, the Commission lodged with the Court a meeting agenda and other materials establishing that at its May 8, 2019 meeting, it made supplemental findings rejecting the City's objections to the Trestle's listing, and again finding that the Trestle meets the criteria for listing on the California Register. On June 12, 2019, the Court entered judgment denying the City's petition for writ of mandate. Real Party in Interest fails to satisfy the threshold prerequisite to an award of attorney's fees: that it must have been the "successful party." (Code Civ. Proc. § 1021.5.) The "successful" party under section 1021.5 is generally the "prevailing party," that is, the party that "succeeds on any significant issue in litigation which achieves some of the benefit the parties sought in bringing suit." (Riverwatch v. County of San Diego Dept. of Environmental Health (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 768, 783, quoting Maria P. v. Riles (1987) 43 Cal.3d 1281, 1291.) "The litigant is considered 'successful' under section 1021.5 if the litigation 'contributed substantially to remedying the conditions at which it was directed.'" (Id., quoting Planned Parenthood v. Aakhus (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 162, 174; see also Save Our Heritage Organisation v. City of San Diego (2017) 11 Cal.App.5th 154, 160.) (END OF PART 1 OF 3) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion, And Motion For A Temporary Stay Of Execution Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 918; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 2, PETITIONER TARA MATERIALS, INC.'s Motion For A Temporary Stay Of Execution Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 918. The court grants Tara Material Inc.'s motion for a temporary stay of execution, which shall expire "10 days beyond the last date on which a notice of appeal could be filed." (Code of Civ. Proc., § 918(b). The court finds good cause for issuance of the stay given the amount of the judgment and difficulty in obtaining a bond of nearly $5,000,000. (Benator Dec., 3.) As a condition of this order, the court orders Tara not to transfer any assets outside the course of ordinary business during the stay's pendency. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Tara is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Entry Of Judgment And Award Of Prejudgment Interest
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 2, RESPONDENT ATI INDUSTRIES, INC.'s Motion For Entry Of Judgment And Award Of Prejudgment Interest. ATI Industries, Inc.'s motion for entry of judgment and motion to recover post-arbitration award and prejudgment interest at a rate of 10% is granted. In Britz, Inc. v. Alfa-Laval Food & Dairy Co. (1995) 34 Cal.App.4th 1085, the court held that a successful party to arbitration is entitled to post-award, pre-judgment interest under Civil Code section 3287, subdivision (a). The Britz court held that a successful party to an arbitration is entitled to "recover damages certain" within the meaning of section 3287, subdivision (a) as of the date the arbitrator renders his or her award. (Id. at 1106.) The court noted that "[t]he arbitration award itself result[s] in a new and fixed liability [citation]. Regardless of the individual elements that comprised that liability, respondents were entitled to payment of the fixed sum upon issuance of the award. [].... Although the interest was pre-'judicial judgment,' it was post-'contractual judgment.' Any result that denied respondents this post-award interest would punish them for using arbitration instead of the court system to resolve their dispute with appellants." (Id. at 1107; see also Tenzera, Inc. v. Osterman (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 16, 21 ["A prevailing party in arbitration is entitled to prejudgment interest as of the date of the final award to entry of judgment"]; Pierotti v. Torian (2000) 81 Cal.App.4th 17, 26-28 [party that prevailed on petition to confirm arbitration award was entitled to post-award, pre-judgment interest as a matter of right].) Cal. Civil Code, § 3289(b) provides: "If a contract entered into after January 1, 1986, does not stipulate a legal rate of interest, the obligation shall bear interest at a rate of 10 percent per annum after a breach." Cal. Code of Civ. Proc., § 1287.6 provides that "[a]n award that has not been confirmed or vacated has the same force and effect as a contract in writing between the parties to the arbitration." ATI is therefore entitled to prejudgment interest at a rate of 10%. It does not matter whether the underlying claims were equitable because under Britz, the liability became fixed and the individual elements that comprised the liability are no longer relevant. The court will execute ATI's proposed form of judgment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for ATI is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JANE DOE (1) ET AL VS. STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 8, RESPONDENT STATE BAR OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION. Defendant State Bar's demurrer to the petition for writ of mandate is continued to December 10, 2019. The demurrer raises an unresolved threshold issue: whether plaintiffs may sue using a fictitious name. This is permitted in "extraordinary circumstances," but a prior court order is required. (See Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2019) § 6.90.10; Starbucks Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1436, 1452 n.7 ("judicial use of Doe plaintiffs"; "order which maintains the confidentiality of John Doe's identity" (emphasis added; quotation marks deleted).) Plaintiffs have not obtained such an order in this case. Plaintiffs shall file and serve a motion to proceed anonymously to be heard in November. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TODD DAVID SPENCER MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment Peremptory Writ
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 3, PETITIONER TODD SPENCER's Motion For Judgment Peremptory Writ. Petitioner's "Notice of Motion and Motion for Judgment Peremptory Writ" is denied. Petitioner fails to demonstrate good cause for a stay of the administrative decision per Code of Civ. Proc., § 1094.5(h). To obtain such a stay, petitioner must demonstrate that the stay will not cause the public interest to suffer and the agency is unlikely to prevail on the merits. (See Board of Medical Quality Assur. v. Superior Court (1980) 114 Cal.App.3d 272, 276.) In this case, petitioner fails to meet either prong of this test. Given petitioner's history of substance abuse, petitioner fails to show that it would be in the public interest to allow him to continue practicing medicine pending disposition of his petition. Petitioner has not shown that the Medical Board is unlikely to succeed on the merits. (See Medical Board of California v. Superior Court (1991) 227 Cal.App.3d 1458, 1460-1462 [trial court abused its discretion in staying administrative order revoking physician's license to practice where physician failed to establish agency was unlikely to prevail].) General contract principles apply to the parties' decision/stipulated settlement and "[a]s other contracts, a stipulation for settlement may be rescinded if it was procured through fraud, duress, undue influence or mistake." (See Stermer v. Board of Dental Examiners (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 128, 133.) Petitioner fails to demonstrate a basis for rescission. Petitioner argues that the testing fees that he must pay are cost prohibitive and since he cannot work, he cannot afford to pay them. This argument founders. Petitioner fails to provide any information regarding his employment efforts or his inability to find work in any of the allowed fields, and he does not establish "mistake." (See Stermer, 95 Cal.App.4th at 133.) The court further notes that petitioner was represented by counsel when he entered into the stipulated settlement; he represented that he had "carefully read, fully discussed with counsel, and understands the effects" of the stipulation; and he expressly waived his rights, including his right to court review. (Stipulated Settlement, pgs. 2-3.) "Stipulations in administrative proceedings would not serve the purpose for which they are intended if they were voidable at the option of the licensee based on changes of circumstances or challenges to factual recitals." (Stermer, 95 Cal.App.4th at 133-134.) Therefore, petitioner's petition for a stay and/or petition for writ of mandate is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL DENNY VS. JOHN ARNTZ ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Respondents' Demurrer To The Petition (Per 9-24-19 Ex Parte Order)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 4, Respondents' Demurrer To The Petition (Per 9-24-19 Ex Parte Order). As a threshold matter, Petitioner Michael Denny's "Request for Disqualification of Judge" contained in his October 4, 2019 opposition to Respondents' demurrer is stricken. The request appears to be based on Petitioner's contention that the Court displayed "bias" against the Petition in two respects: (1) by failing to give the petition priority over all other civil matters, as required by the Elections Code; and (2) by granting Respondent City's request for a hearing date that, he contends, "is likely to prove fatal to the Petition." Neither contention constitutes a basis for a peremptory or cause challenge to Judge Schulman hearing the matter, and neither has any merit in any event. The Court's full tentative ruling is emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SEUNGMIN SAMANTHA LEE VS. INFOR (US), INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Final Approval Of Class Action Settlement, Class Representatives Service Award, And Attorneys' Fees And Costs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF SEUNGMIN LEE's Motion For Final Approval Of Class Action Settlement, Class Representatives Service Award, And Attorneys' Fees And Costs. Plaintiffs' motion for final approval of proposed class action settlement and class representative's service award is granted. No opposition filed, and good cause shown. The Court defers ruling on Plaintiff's motion for an award of attorneys' fees and costs, pending submission by class counsel of a supplemental declaration sufficient to show counsel's lodestar so that the Court may conduct a cross-check of the reasonableness of the requested percentage-based fee. (See Laffitte v. Robert Half International, Inc. (2016) 1 Cal.5th 480, 503-506.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: NEXTARTS, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. ROBERT R SCHMIDT ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, October 11, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT ROBERT SCHMIDT, ROBERT'S TAX SERVICE, LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The demurrer of Defendants Robert R. Schmidt and Robert's Tax Service, LLC to Plaintiff NextArts's first amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend on the ground that it is barred in its entirety by the statute of limitations. The Court does not reach the additional grounds for the demurrer raised for the first time, improperly, in Defendants' reply brief, but not in their opening brief or in the notice of demurrer. (See Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1538 ["'[p]oints raised for the first time in a reply brief will ordinarily not be considered, because such consideration would deprive the respondent of an opportunity to counter the argument.'"].) (The Court's full tentative ruling was emailed to the parties.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JUAN RIVAS JIMENEZ VS. EL RINCONCITO CATRACHO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form And Special Interrogatories, Sets One; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiffs Attorneys In The Sum Of $1,092
rulings.Rulings: OCTOBER 11, 2019 LINE 2, DEFENDANT MARK BRENNAN'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form And Special Interrogatories, Sets One; Request For Order Awarding Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiffs Attorneys In The Sum Of $1,092 Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED. The moving party failed to fully engage in a good faith meet and confer effort prior to pursuing this motion. "The Discovery Act requires that, prior to the initiation of a motion to compel, the moving party declare that he or she has made a serious attempt to obtain an informal resolution of each issue." (Townsend v. Superior Court (1998) 61 Cal. App. 4th 1431, 1435.) "This rule is designed to encourage the parties to work out their differences informally so as to avoid the necessity for a formal order . . . lessen[ing] the burden on the court and reduc[ing] the unnecessary expenditure of resources by litigants through promotion of informal, extrajudicial resolution of discovery disputes." (Stewart v. Colonial W. Agency, Inc. (2001) 87 Cal. App. 4th 1006, 1016.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/302).

rulings.Case Title: ARMANDO REYES VS. STARBUCKS CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Defendants Starbucks Corporation And Jen Lu To Further Respond To (1) His Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents And (2) For The Award Of Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: OCTOBER 11, 2019 LINE 3, PLAINTIFF ARMANDO REYES' Motion For Order Compelling Defendants Starbucks Corporation And Jen Lu To Further Respond To (1) His Demand For Production And Inspection Of Documents And (2) For The Award Of Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Noah Lebowitz, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED. The Plaintiff's filings failed to comply with California Rule of Court 3.1113 in multiple respects including, but not limited to, vastly exceeding the page limits. Additionally, the Defendant demonstrated it had served code-compliant responses to the discovery at issue and no further production of documents is required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to noah@ndllegal.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (JPT/302).
